verses.text: The burden of 'the valley of vision': the city is full of noise and revelry, yet Isaiah weeps bitterly over the spoiling of his people. Mirth covers coming ruin. The prophet grieves where the people only make merry.

The burden of 'the valley of vision': the city is full of noise and revelry, yet Isaiah weeps bitterly over the spoiling of his people. Mirth covers coming ruin. The prophet grieves where the people only make merry.

It is a day of trouble and treading down, the valleys filled with enemy chariots at the gate. Judgment presses in. The defences men trusted are overrun in the day of the LORD's reckoning.

They look to their armour and store water for the siege, but 'have not looked unto the maker thereof.' They trust their works, not God. Preparation without prayer forgets the One who alone can save.

God called for weeping and mourning, but they answered with feasting: 'let us eat and drink; for to morrow we shall die.' Reckless pleasure defies His call. To meet warning with revelry is a sin not purged till death.

Shebna, the proud steward who carved himself a fine tomb, is told God will hurl him away and strip his office. Self-glory is brought low. God removes the self-important from the place they exalted themselves in.

In his place God calls faithful Eliakim, laying 'the key of the house of David upon his shoulder,' to open and shut. He is given true authority. This key points to Christ, who alone opens and none can shut.

In his place God calls faithful Eliakim, laying 'the key of the house of David upon his shoulder,' to open and shut. He is given true authority. This key points to Christ, who alone opens and none can shut.
Eliakim is fastened 'as a nail in a sure place,' a glory to his house; yet even that nail shall at last be removed. No human prop is permanent. Only Christ, the true and lasting Nail, never fails.
